title: Gap Fill: The Discovery of Penicillin

tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst, z ktorego usunieto szesc zdan. Dobierz zdania oznaczone literami (A-H) tak, aby tekst byl spojny i logiczny. Dwa zdania zostaly podane dodatkowo i nie pasuja do zadnego fragmentu.
tasks.text:
THE DISCOVERY OF PENICILLIN

Few scientific breakthroughs have owed as much to chance as the discovery of penicillin. In the summer of 1928, Alexander Fleming, a Scottish bacteriologist working at St Mary's Hospital in London, left a stack of petri dishes on his laboratory bench before departing for a holiday. _________ (1.1) Rather than discarding the contaminated dish, Fleming examined it more closely and made an observation that would transform the course of medicine.

Around the bluish-green mould, there was a clear ring where the staphylococcus bacteria had been completely destroyed. Fleming deduced that the mould was secreting a substance lethal to bacteria, and he named it penicillin. _________ (1.2) The mould was notoriously difficult to cultivate under controlled conditions, and the active compound broke down rapidly once extracted.

Fleming published a paper on his findings in 1929, but it attracted remarkably little attention from the wider medical community. _________ (1.3) It was not until a decade later that two Oxford researchers, Howard Florey and Ernst Chain, revisited his work with the resources and expertise needed to develop the drug.

Florey and Chain faced enormous technical challenges. Producing even small quantities of purified penicillin required vast amounts of mould culture. _________ (1.4) Their persistence paid off in 1941, when penicillin was used to treat a policeman suffering from a severe bacterial infection, with remarkable initial results.

The outbreak of World War Two created an urgent demand for an effective antibacterial drug. Battlefield wounds frequently became infected, and existing treatments were inadequate. _________ (1.5) By D-Day in June 1944, enough penicillin was available to treat every Allied soldier who needed it.

In 1945, Fleming, Florey, and Chain shared the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. In his acceptance speech, Fleming issued a prescient warning about bacteria developing resistance if antibiotics were used carelessly. _________ (1.6) The search for new antibiotics to replace those that bacteria have learned to resist is now one of the most pressing challenges in global health.

options.text: Upon his return, he noticed that a mould had drifted onto one of the dishes containing colonies of Staphylococcus aureus.
However, turning this laboratory curiosity into a usable medicine proved far more difficult than anyone had anticipated.
At the time, most scientists regarded mould contamination as a nuisance rather than a potential source of therapeutic agents.
They devised an ingenious method of growing the mould in hundreds of ceramic bedpans to increase the yield of the precious substance.
American pharmaceutical companies were recruited to scale up production using deep-tank fermentation, a technique borrowed from the brewing industry.
That warning, largely ignored for decades, now seems alarmingly accurate as antibiotic-resistant infections spread worldwide.
Fleming had previously served as a battlefield medic during the First World War, where he witnessed countless soldiers die from infected wounds.
Several other naturally occurring antibiotics were discovered in soil bacteria during the 1940s, including streptomycin and chloramphenicol.
